ACG Clinical Guideline: Colonic Diverticulitis
  • Source: American Journal of Gastroenterology, Vol. 121, July 2026
  • Topic: Management of colonic diverticulitis in outpatient gastroenterology settings
  • Author(s): Peery AF, Strate LL, Stollman N, Mankaney G, Chang JW, Grover S
1. Disease Overview & Epidemiology
  • Lifetime risk: 3% in men, 5% in women
  • Annual US healthcare burden:
  • 1.7 million clinic visits
  • 452,000 emergency department visits
  • 179,000 hospital admissions
  • 33,000 elective + ~33,000 nonelective operations
  • Healthcare cost: $5.5 billion annually in the US
  • Deaths: 5,754 per year (infrequently fatal)
  • Most common presentation --> acute uncomplicated diverticulitis (~88% of cases)
1.1 Definitions
  • Diverticulosis: Outpouchings where mucosa/submucosa herniate through muscularis propria at vasa recta penetration points
  • Acute uncomplicated diverticulitis: Localized inflammation without abscess, perforation, stricture, or fistula
  • Complicated diverticulitis: Inflammation with phlegmon, abscess, perforation, peritonitis, stricture, or fistula
  • Smoldering/chronic diverticulitis: Inflammation persisting weeks to months with partial/complete symptom improvement then relapse after antibiotics stopped
2. Complicated Diverticulitis Classification (Modified Hinchey)
  • Stage 0: Mild clinical diverticulitis (wall thickening, pericolic inflammation)
  • Stage Ia: Confined pericolic inflammation or phlegmon
  • Stage Ib: Pericolic or mesocolic abscess
  • Stage II: Pelvic/intra-abdominal/retroperitoneal abscess
  • Stage III: Generalized purulent peritonitis
  • Stage IV: Generalized fecal peritonitis
2.1 Clinical Implications
  • Stages 0–I --> typically managed nonoperatively
  • Higher stages often require drainage or surgery
  • Abscess is most common complication
3. Diagnosis & Clinical Course
3.1 CT Imaging Essential Role
  • Confirms suspected diverticulitis (especially first presentation)
  • Excludes alternative diagnoses
  • Grades disease severity to guide treatment
  • Identifies affected location for surgical planning
3.2 Clinical Features Increasing Diagnosis Likelihood
  • Older age
  • Previous episodes
  • Left lower abdominal pain and tenderness
  • Pain with movement
  • Absence of vomiting
  • Elevated C-reactive protein
3.3 Risk Patterns
Pattern Statistic
Diverticulosis --> diverticulitis risk 1%–4% over 7 years
Complicated disease risk Highest at initial presentation, decreases with recurrences
Recurrence after 1st episode (10-year) At least 22%
Recurrence after 2nd episode (10-year) At least 55%
Recurrence after ≥3 episodes Risk remains high
Elective surgery recurrence (5-year) ~15% vs ~60% without surgery
3.4 Chronic Gastrointestinal Symptoms
  • Common after recovery: >1/3 of patients report persistent symptoms at 1–2 years
  • Includes abdominal pain and altered bowel habits
  • May represent functional gastrointestinal disorders post-diverticulitis
4. Colonoscopy After Diverticulitis Recovery
4.1 Complicated Diverticulitis (Strong Recommendation)
  • Recommend colonoscopy to rule out missed cancer/premalignant lesion
  • Colon cancer risk: OR 5.65 (95% CI 2.95–10.8) vs uncomplicated
  • Advanced neoplasia risk: OR 3.85 (95% CI 2.11–7.03)
4.2 Uncomplicated Diverticulitis (Conditional Recommendation)
  • Suggest colonoscopy if:
  • Alarm symptoms present (unintentional weight loss, bowel habit changes, iron-deficiency anemia, bloody stools, persistent pain)
  • Not current with colorectal cancer screening
4.3 Timing & Safety
  • Avoid colonoscopy within 6–8 weeks of acute episode
  • Incomplete colonoscopy risk: 3.7% (95% CI 2.7%–4.9%)
  • Perforation risk: 0.2%
5. Antibiotic Management
5.1 Routine Antibiotics Not Recommended for Low-Risk Patients
  • Conditional recommendation against routine antibiotics for acute uncomplicated diverticulitis in low-risk patients
  • Evidence from 4 RCTs (1,809 patients) shows antibiotics had little/no effect on:
  • Mortality: RR 0.22–4.82
  • Progression to complicated disease: RR 0.35–1.50
  • Need for surgery: RR 0.35–1.44
  • Readmission: RR 0.47–1.58
  • Recurrent diverticulitis: RR 0.67–1.34
5.2 When Antibiotics Are Appropriate
  • Immunocompromise
  • Frailty
  • Significant comorbidities
  • Clinical deterioration or persistent symptoms
  • Inability to tolerate oral intake
  • Vomiting
  • Elevated inflammatory markers (e.g., CRP >140 mg/L)
  • Imaging features: fluid collection, longer inflammation segment
  • Inadequate outpatient support or inability to ensure close follow-up
5.3 Outpatient Regimens
  • Monotherapy options:
  • Amoxicillin-clavulanic acid 875 mg/125 mg orally every 12 hours
  • Moxifloxacin 400 mg orally daily
  • Combination therapy:
  • Metronidazole 500 mg every 8 hours + (TMP-SMX 160/800 mg every 12 hours OR ciprofloxacin 500 mg every 12 hours OR levofloxacin 500 mg daily)
  • Duration: 4–7 days
6. Lifestyle Modifications for Recurrence Prevention
6.1 Healthy Diet (Conditional Recommendation)
  • Suggest high-fiber diet rich in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, legumes
  • Limit: red meat, processed grains, trans fats, sweets
6.1.1 Evidence Summary
  • Prudent diet pattern: HR 0.74 (95% CI 0.60–0.91) for highest vs lowest quintile
  • DASH diet: HR 0.77 (95% CI 0.65–0.90)
  • Health Eating Index: HR 0.78 (95% CI 0.66–0.91)
  • Alternative Healthy Eating Index: HR 0.81 (95% CI 0.69–0.95)
  • Vegetarian vs meat eaters: HR 0.69 (31% lower risk)
  • High fiber intake: HR 0.59 (41% lower risk for highest vs lowest quintile)
  • High red meat intake: HR 1.58 (95% CI 1.19–2.11)
6.2 Nuts, Seeds, Corn, Popcorn (NOT Necessary to Avoid)
  • Suggest against avoidance of these foods to reduce recurrence risk
  • Nuts: HR 0.80 (95% CI 0.63–1.01) for highest vs lowest intake
  • Popcorn: HR 0.72 (95% CI 0.52–0.92)
  • Corn: HR 1.13 (95% CI 0.83–1.54) - no increased risk
  • No evidence supports avoiding these foods
6.3 NSAIDs (Conditional Recommendation)
  • Suggest avoid regular NSAID use after recovery to reduce recurrence risk
  • Nonaspirin NSAIDs: HR 1.72 (95% CI 1.40–2.11) for incident diverticulitis
  • Aspirin: HR 1.25 (95% CI 1.05–1.47)
  • Do NOT avoid if compelling indication for use
6.4 Smoking Cessation (Conditional Recommendation)
  • Suggest smoking cessation to reduce recurrence risk
  • Current smokers: RR 1.36 (95% CI 1.15–1.61) for diverticular disease
  • Current smokers: RR 2.54 (95% CI 1.49–4.33) for complicated diverticulitis
6.5 Alcohol Moderation (Conditional Recommendation)
  • Suggest moderation for heavy alcohol users
  • >30 g/day (>2 drinks/day): HR 1.26 (95% CI 1.05–1.50) for incident diverticulitis
  • Combined smoking + ≥15 g/day alcohol: 60% higher risk
6.6 Weight Loss (Conditional Recommendation)
  • Suggest weight loss for overweight/obese patients
  • BMI ≥30 vs <22.5: HR 1.66 (95% CI 1.09–2.51) for recurrent diverticulitis
  • Visceral adiposity may be more important risk factor than overall body fat
6.7 Physical Activity (Conditional Recommendation)
  • Suggest regular physical activity to reduce recurrence risk
  • High vs low activity: RR 0.76 (95% CI 0.63–0.93) for incident diverticulitis
6.8 Combined Lifestyle Impact
  • Each low-risk lifestyle factor associated with ~50% reduction in incident diverticulitis risk
  • Benefits extend regardless of genetic susceptibility
7. Medications for Recurrence Prevention
7.1 Mesalamine (Strong Recommendation AGAINST)
  • Recommend against mesalamine to reduce recurrence
  • Moderate certainty evidence
  • Largest trials (PREVENT-1, PREVENT-2) showed no efficacy
  • Meta-analysis of 6 RCTs (1,898 patients): OR 1.15 (95% CI 0.92–1.44) for recurrence
  • More discontinuation due to adverse events: OR 1.59 (95% CI 1.12–2.27)
7.2 Rifaximin (Conditional Recommendation AGAINST)
  • Recommend against rifaximin to reduce recurrence
  • Very low certainty evidence
  • Single small trial showed benefit but was:
  • Open-label
  • Unclear outcome definitions
  • Stopped early due to poor recruitment
7.3 Probiotics (Conditional Recommendation AGAINST)
  • Recommend against probiotics to reduce recurrence
  • Very low certainty evidence
  • Single RCT showed no benefit (mesalamine + probiotics vs placebo)
  • Cost and burden considerations
8. Surgical Considerations (Recurrent Uncomplicated Disease)
8.1 Indications for Referral
  • Recurrent, uncomplicated diverticulitis significantly impairing quality of life
  • Conditional recommendation for surgical consultation
8.2 Surgery NOT Indicated For:
  • Young age alone
  • Certain number of episodes alone
  • Prevention of future emergency
8.3 Evidence Summary
Factor Finding
Recurrence reduction Surgery reduces but does NOT eliminate risk
5-year recurrence ~15% with surgery vs ~60% without
Quality of life (DIRECT, LASER trials) Improved with surgery BUT 30% in DIRECT had no improvement or worse
Short-term complications 10%–34% (surgical site infections, iatrogenic injury, anastomotic leak)
Long-term complications Incisional hernia, permanent ostomy, strictures, adhesive small bowel obstruction, new-onset bowel dysfunction
8.4 Pre-Surgery Requirements
  • CT confirmation of diverticulitis before elective resection
  • Patients with chronic symptoms should have realistic expectations (pain may persist)
  • In LASER trial: 73% had baseline abdominal pain, 63% still had pain at 6 months post-op
9. Related Conditions
9.1 SCAD (Segmental Colitis Associated with Diverticulosis)
  • Chronic inflammatory condition affecting colonic segments with diverticula
  • Typically spares rectum
  • Inflammation centered on/around diverticula
  • Histology can resemble ulcerative colitis or Crohn's disease
  • Unclear if distinct entity or manifestation of IBD
9.2 SUDD (Symptomatic Uncomplicated Diverticular Disease)
  • Controversial diagnosis
  • Bloating, chronic/recurrent abdominal pain, or altered bowel habits in diverticulosis
  • Absence of systemic inflammation or overt diverticulitis
10. Key Clinical Decision Points Summary
10.1 Antibiotic Decision Tree

```

Acute Uncomplicated Diverticulitis Patient

↓

All criteria met? (immunocompetent, outpatient, no complications,

hemodynamically stable, no SIRS, not frail, oral intake OK,

no recent antibiotics, reliable follow-up)

↓

YES → Consider NO antibiotics (close follow-up)

NO → Prescribe antibiotics

```

10.2 Colonoscopy Decision Tree

```

Post-Diverticulitis Patient

↓

Complicated diverticulitis? → YES → Colonoscopy recommended (Strong)

NO → Alarm symptoms or not current with screening?

↓

YES → Colonoscopy suggested (Conditional)

NO → Individualize based on patient factors

```

10.3 Surgery Decision Tree

```

Recurrent Uncomplicated Diverticulitis

↓

Significantly affects quality of life?

↓

YES → Refer for surgical consultation (discuss risks/benefits)

NO → Continue conservative management + lifestyle modifications

```

11. Patient Education Key Points
11.1 What Patients Should Know
  • Lifetime risk ~3%–5%
  • Constipation does NOT cause diverticulitis (no need for laxatives unless indicated)
  • Nuts/seeds/popcorn/corn do NOT need to be avoided
  • Clear liquid diet may help during acute episode (weeks 1–2)
  • High-fiber foods (fruits, vegetables, whole grains, beans) may help prevent recurrence
  • Add fiber slowly over time (too fast causes cramping)
  • Supplemental fiber is NOT substitute for healthy diet
11.2 Prevention Summary
  • Stay physically active
  • Maintain healthy weight
  • Don't smoke
  • Limit alcohol
  • Avoid regular NSAIDs when possible
11.3 When to Seek Care During Acute Episode
  • Worsening symptoms
  • Inability to tolerate oral fluids
  • High fever
  • Signs of complications
12. Research Gaps Identified by Guideline
12.1 Needed Advances
  • Better prediction tools (clinical, biomarker, genetic, imaging-based)
  • Identify who will recur or develop complications
  • Determine who benefits most from antibiotics or elective surgery
  • High-quality trials on dietary/lifestyle interventions for recurrence prevention
  • Evidence to guide what/when patients should eat during acute uncomplicated episode
  • Comparative trials of elective surgery vs conservative management in real-world populations
  • Long-term quality of life and decision regret data
  • Validated decision aids for patients considering elective surgery
